mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW M850i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned N63 engine typically costs £9,000–£17,000 supply only, with used units available from £3,000–£6,500. Fitting adds £2,000–£3,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW M850i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N63 has a notorious reputation. Early versions (2008–2013) were genuinely unreliable — timing chain failures, excessive oil consumption, and turbocharger issues were common. The M850i's N63TU3 is the most refined iteration, with many issues addressed. However, diligent maintenance — especially frequent oil changes (3,500–5,000 miles) — is absolutely essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW M850i?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain stretch and guide failure (the most severe issue), excessive oil consumption and valve stem seal leaks, turbocharger failure, and cooling system problems. The 2019 model also had a high-pressure fuel pump recall.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW M850i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: M850i values remain strong — a well-kept example is often worth £30,000–£60,000+. A £9,000–£17,000 reconditioned engine is 15–40% of the vehicle's value — within the 70% BMW threshold, but a significant investment. Replacement only makes sense if the rest of the car is in excellent condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW M850i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is N63B44T3 or N63B44D — it's the only engine used in the G14/G15/G16 M850i. You can find it on the VIN plate, in the vehicle documentation, or on a label on the engine itself.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N63 timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: It needs replacement — the chain guides break and the tensioner wears. A full timing chain, tensioner, guide and VANOS sprocket replacement costs £3,000–£6,000. It's a labour-intensive job but far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£9,000+). At the first sign of rattling from the front of the engine, book an inspection immediately.

eraMap.title: The M850i THROUGH TIME — Era Map

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current M850i market. A replacement N63B44D motor costs approximately £12,525 on average, with new or reconditioned units reaching up to £16,819. Fitting adds £2,000–£3,000. See the Era Map above for generation-specific context.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an M850i V8 engine typically takes 16–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The N63's "hot-vee" design makes some repairs exceptionally labour-intensive.

cta.label: → Compare M850i engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch and Guide Failure
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking from the front of the engine, engine warning light, cam timing errors, eventual chain slip
problems.repairCost: £3,000–£6,000 (timing chain, tensioner, guides and VANOS sprocket replacement)
problems.replacementCost: £9,000–£13,000 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: any rattling from the front of the engine on an N63 requires immediate investigation

problems.recommendation: The N63's timing chain guides are made of brittle plastic that can break apart, sending metal fragments through the engine. The "hot-vee" design generates extreme heat loads that accelerate component wear. This is one of the most severe and common problems on the N63. BMW faced class action lawsuits over N63 timing chain and oil consumption issues. If you hear any rattling from the front of the engine, book an inspection immediately. A preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Excessive Oil Consumption and Valve Stem Seal Leaks
problems.symptoms: Oil level dropping between services, blue smoke on cold start or after idling, low oil pressure warning, puff of smoke when accelerating

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: the N63 can burn up to a quart of oil in 1,000 miles

problems.recommendation: The N63 is notorious for excessive oil consumption. This is caused by valve stem seal leaks, turbocharger oil seal failure, and PCV system issues. BMW even launched a Customer Care Package program to address widespread issues. Check oil levels regularly — if you're topping up more than 1L between services, have the valve stem seals and turbochargers inspected. Ignoring oil consumption can lead to catastrophic engine failure.

failureLink.label: Read the full valve stem seal failure guide

problems.title: Turbocharger Failure and Cooling System Issues
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, whistling or grinding noise from the engine bay, blue or black smoke, engine warning light, overheating
problems.recommendation: The N63's twin turbochargers are crammed into a tight engine bay, generating extreme heat loads that overwhelm cooling systems and accelerate component wear. The 2019 M850i also had a recall for high-pressure fuel pump flanges that could crack, causing fuel leaks. Extended oil change intervals (recommended by BMW) cause coking issues in the hot-vee engine. Many specialists recommend oil changes every 3,500–5,000 miles instead. If you notice any whistling, loss of power, or overheating, have the turbochargers and cooling system inspected immediately.

failureLink.label: Read the full turbocharger failure guide

buyingChecks.title: Buying This M850i? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with frequent oil changes (3,500–5,000 mile intervals — not BMW's extended schedule)
* Evidence of timing chain inspection or replacement
* No blue smoke on cold start — valve stem seals should be healthy
* A well-maintained example where the previous owner understood the N63's needs
avoidIf: * Any rattling from the front of the engine — timing chain issues are likely
* Blue smoke on cold start — valve stem seals are failing
* Poor service history — the N63 is unforgiving of neglect
* Evidence of oil consumption without a diagnosed fix
* Any engine warning light without a clear diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The M850i is a genuine grand tourer — 530 hp, 750 Nm, 0–62 mph in 3.9 seconds. The N63 V8 that powers it is a masterpiece of engineering, but one with a notorious reputation. Early N63s (2008–2013) were genuinely unreliable. The M850i's N63TU3 is the most refined iteration, but it still carries the hot-vee V8's legacy. Buy on history, maintain religiously (oil changes every 3,500–5,000 miles), and the M850i will reward you with one of BMW's finest driving experiences. Skip the history and it will punish you.

cta.label: → Compare M850i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW M850i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any blue smoke? (valve stem seal warning — a critical check on the N63)
2. Rattling — Any rattling from the front of the engine? (timing chain warning)
3. Oil Consumption — How much oil does it use between services? (N63s are known for oil thirst)
4. Service History — including evidence of frequent oil changes (3,500–5,000 mile intervals recommended)
5. Coolant Level — Any unexplained loss? (cooling system warning)
6. Turbo Performance — Test acceleration; any whistling, hesitation, or loss of power?
7. Recall Check — Has the high-pressure fuel pump recall been completed? (2019 models)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (catalyst, O2 sensors)
